title: Spaghetti alla carbonara
subtitle: Spaghetti with guanciale, egg yolk, and pecorino
excerpt: Spaghetti tossed with rendered guanciale and a sauce of egg yolks, whole eggs, and pecorino romano — no cream, no garlic, no onion. The Roman pasta that requires a specific technique to turn raw eggs into a creamy coating without scrambling them.
sourceNotes: Based on the Roman carbonara tradition as documented in Anna Del Conte, 'Gastronomy of Italy' (1987) and Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006). The canonical formula uses guanciale (cured pork cheek), pecorino romano, whole eggs and egg yolks, black pepper, and spaghetti — nothing else.

content.text: Carbonara is one of the most technically precise pasta dishes and one of the most frequently misunderstood. The sauce is not cream; it is eggs. The technique is to warm the egg mixture to the point where it thickens from the starch in the pasta water and the fat from the guanciale into a glossy, coating sauce — without curdling. The margin between perfectly creamy and scrambled is narrow and achieved by removing the pan from the heat and working quickly.

Guanciale is the correct meat: fattier and more savoury than pancetta, with a different flavour after rendering. Pancetta is a good substitute; streaky bacon is a distant one. Pecorino romano is the correct cheese — salty, sharp, and harder than parmesan. A 50/50 blend of pecorino and parmesan is common in Rome.

items.prepNote: rigatoni is traditional for the Rome restaurant version; spaghetti for the home kitchen
cut into lardons; pancetta as substitute
finely grated; or 40 g pecorino + 40 g parmesan
coarsely ground — this is a flavour, not a seasoning
for pasta water only — the guanciale and cheese provide all other salt

content.text: Put {{guanciale}} of guanciale lardons in a cold frying pan. Heat over a medium heat and cook until the fat is rendered and the guanciale is golden and slightly crisp — about 8 minutes. Remove from the heat. Do not drain the fat.

Whisk {{eggs}} whole eggs, {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks, and {{pecorino}} most of the grated pecorino together in a bowl with {{black-pepper-ground}} of coarsely ground black pepper. The mixture should be thick and pale yellow.

Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a large mug of pasta water before draining. Immediately add the drained pasta to the guanciale pan off the heat. Toss to coat in the fat. Add a large ladleful of pasta water and toss for 30 seconds to cool the pasta slightly. Add the egg mixture and toss constantly and quickly, adding pasta water a tablespoon at a time to maintain a flowing, creamy consistency. The eggs should thicken without scrambling — work entirely off the heat. Serve immediately with the remaining pecorino.

items.cause: Pan still too hot when egg mixture was added
items.fix: Wait 30 seconds after turning off the heat and adding the first pasta water before adding the eggs. Toss constantly. If eggs are scrambling, add cold pasta water immediately.

items.symptom: Sauce is too liquid and watery
items.cause: Too much pasta water added, or eggs too cool
items.fix: Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time. The sauce thickens from the egg and starch together — less water is needed than you might think.

items.symptom: Sauce is gluey and thick rather than creamy
items.fix: Add pasta water and toss vigorously. The starch in the water emulsifies with the fat to thin the sauce.

content.text: Carbonara is Roman — specifically from the Lazio culinary tradition, alongside cacio e pepe, amatriciana, and gricia, which together form the four canonical pasta dishes of Rome. Its exact origin is uncertain: the dish appears in Roman cookbooks from the late 1940s and is sometimes associated with American troops and dried egg rations during the Second World War, though this is disputed. What is not disputed is that cream, never part of the original, crept in through restaurant adaptations outside Italy and became the dominant British and American version. The authentic preparation requires no cream.
